title: FIFA World Cup 2026 Squads: Complete Official 48-Team Lists
original_text:
The wait is finally over, the FIFA World Cup 2026 Squads are finally here. It is not just another tournament; it is a historic footballing revolution. For the first time in the competition’s 96-year history, 48 nations are set to battle across three host countries —the USA, Mexico, and Canada.

This expansion has opened the doors for over 1,000 professional footballers to represent their flags on the grandest stage of all. From world-class superstars to determined debutants, the FIFA World Cup 2026 Squads represent the most diverse and competitive pool of talent ever assembled in sports history.

FIFA World Cup 2026 squads rules allow each of the 48 qualified national teams to register at least 23 and at most 26 players (including three required goalkeepers). A provisional list of 35 to 55 players was submitted on May 11, 2026 , ahead of the final squad locks on June 1, 2026.

This edition marks a fascinating transition period for global football. While veteran icons like Lionel Messi and Cristiano Ronaldo look to etch their names into eternity for one last time, a new era of ‘wonderkids’ is ready to take the mantle.

Teams like Spain , led by the likes of Lamine Yamal , and France, spearheaded by Kylian Mbappé , are bringing a blend of youthful exuberance and tactical maturity.

Every FIFA World Cup 2026 players list announcement in this guide reflects a careful balance between experienced leaders and the rising stars who will define the next decade of the beautiful game.

Navigating through 48 different team rosters can be overwhelming for any football fan. That is why we have compiled this definitive hub for every single FIFA World Cup 2026 Squad .

We go beyond listing names, breaking down tactical formations, giving injury updates, and spotlighting the ‘X-factor’ players in every group.

Whether you are tracking the powerhouse teams of Group C or the underdog stories emerging from Group A , this real-time guide is the only resource you need to stay ahead of the action in North America.

Group A matches will take place from June 11 to June 24, 2026, across stadiums in Mexico City, Zapopan, and Atlanta.

Mexico revealed a 55-man preliminary squad on May 12, with the final roster set to be announced on June 1.

South Korea revealed their final World Cup squad on May 16.

South Africa is set to reveal their final squad on May 27, but has announced a 32-man preliminary squad on May 21.

Group B matches will take place from June 12 to June 24, 2026, across stadiums in Toronto, Santa Clara, Inglewood, and Vancouver.

Canada announced a preliminary squad of 32 players on May 2 5, with the final squad revealed on May 29 .

Switzerland revealed their final squad via a social media campaign on May 18 and 19.

On May 12, Qatar announced a preliminary squad of 34 players. This was expanded to 35 players on May 18 and then reduced to 28 players on May 25.

Group C matches will take place from June 13 to June 24, 2026, across stadiums in Foxborough, East Rutherford, Philadelphia, Miami Gardens, and Atlanta.

Brazil revealed their 55-man preliminary squad on May 12 and final squad on May 18.

Morocco revealed their final squad on May 26.

Scotland revealed their final squad on May 19.

Haiti revealed their final squad on May 15.

Group D matches will take place from June 12 to June 25, 2026, across stadiums in Inglewood, Vancouver, Santa Clara, and Seattle.

The United States is set to reveal their USMNT final squad on May 26.

Australia has announced their final squad on June 1st.

On May 12, Paraguay revealed a preliminary squad of 55 players. The final squad was announced on June 1.

On May 18, Turkey revealed a preliminary squad of 35 players. Turkey’s final squad was announced on June 2.

Group E matches will take place from June 14 to June 25, 2026, across stadiums in Philadelphia, Houston, Toronto, Kansas City, and East Rutherford.

Germany revealed their final squad on May 21.

Ecuador announced their final squad on May 31.

Ivory Coast revealed their final squad on May 15.

Curaçao revealed their final squad on May 18.

Group F matches will take place from June 14 to June 25, 2026, across stadiums in Arlington, Houston, Guadalupe, and Kansas City.

The Netherlands is set to reveal their final squad on May 27.

Japan revealed their final squad on May 15.

The Tunisia soccer team revealed their final squad on May 15.

The Swedish soccer team revealed their final lineup on May 12.

Group G matches will take place from June 15 to June 26, 2026, across stadiums in Inglewood, Seattle, and Vancouver.

The Belgium soccer team revealed their final squad on May 15.

Iran unveiled a 30-man preliminary squad on May 17, with the final roster set to be announced on June 1.

On May 20, Egypt announced a preliminary squad of 27 players, while the final squad will be revealed on May 29.

New Zealand revealed their final squad on May 14.

Group H matches will take place from June 15 to June 26, 2026, across stadiums in Miami Gardens, Atlanta, Houston, and Zapopan.

Spain announced their final squad on May 25th.

Saudi Arabia is set to reveal its 30-man preliminary squad on May 23.

Cape Verde revealed their final squad on May 18.

Group I matches will take place from June 16 to June 26, 2026, across stadiums in East Rutherford, Foxborough, Philadelphia, and Toronto.

France revealed their final squad on May 14.

Senegal is set to reveal their 28-man preliminary squad on May 21.

Norway is set to reveal their final squad on May 21.

On May 19, Iraq revealed a 34-man preliminary squad , with the final lineup have announced on June 1.

Group J matches will take place from June 16 to June 27, 2026, across stadiums in Kansas City, Santa Clara, and Arlington.

Argentina announced a 55-man preliminary squad on May 11 and revealed the final 26-man squad on May 28.

Austria revealed their final squad on May 18.

Algeria is set to reveal their final squad on May 31.

On May 17, Jordan revealed a 30-man preliminary squad. Their final squad was announced on June 2.

Group K matches will take place from June 17 to June 27, 2026, across stadiums in Houston, Mexico City, Zapopan, Miami Gardens, and Atlanta.

Portugal revealed their final squad on May 19.

Colombia announced a preliminary squad of 55 players on May 14. The final squad announcement is set for May 25.

On May 5, Uzbekistan revealed a preliminary squad of 40 players. The final squad was revealed on June 2.

The DR Congo revealed their final squad on May 18.

Group L matches will take place from June 17 to June 27, 2026, across stadiums in Toronto, Arlington, Foxborough, East Rutherford, and Philadelphia.

The England Soccer team will reveal their final squad on May 22.

Croatia revealed their final squad on May 18.

Panama will announce their final squad on May 26th.

On May 25, Ghana announced a preliminary squad of 28 players, with the final squad revealed on June 1.

Looking for the full match-ups? Check our World Cup 2026 Full Schedule . Here is when every major nation is expected to confirm their squad:

For the 2026 edition, FIFA has maintained the expanded squad size. Each of the 48 nations can name a final roster of 26 players . This allows managers more flexibility to manage player fatigue and tactical changes throughout the 104-match tournament.

National associations must submit their final 26-man lists to FIFA at least 10 days before the opening match (which is June 1, 2026). However, preliminary lists of up to 55 players must be submitted much earlier, by mid-May.

Yes. According to FIFA rules, a player on the final list can only be replaced in the event of a serious injury or illness up to 24 hours before the team’s first match . The replacement must come from the initial preliminary list of 55 players and be certified by the FIFA Medical Committee.

FIFA expanded the tournament to 48 teams to provide more opportunities for nations across all continents to participate. This expansion has specifically benefited AFC (Asia), CAF (Africa), and CONCACAF (North America) by nearly doubling their qualifying slots, leading to several debutants in 2026.

The 48-team format has opened the door for history. Nations like Uzbekistan, Jordan, Curaçao, and Cabo Verde have secured their first-ever World Cup appearances, making the 2026 squads some of the most diverse in football history.
